title: GenAI music firm Suno raises $250m at $2.45bn valuation - Music Ally
text:
Being sued by the music industry’s three biggest rightsholders is clearly no barrier to raising money. GenAI music startup Suno has just announced a $250m Series-C funding round that values the company at $2.45bn.
The round was led by investment firm Menlo Ventures, with tech firm Nvidia’s VC arm NVentures, Hallwood Media, Lightspeed and Matrix chipping in.
Hallwood Media is the label launched by former Geffen exec Neil Jacobsen, which has
signed prominent Suno users
and is behind one of the first GenAI projects to achieve chart success in the US:
Xania Monet
.
“This funding enables us to accelerate what we’re building: more sophisticated tools for professionals, more delightful experiences for casual creators, and new ways for people to share and connect socially through music,”
claimed Suno in a blog post announcing today’s fundin
g.
It also claimed that “nearly 100 million people” have made music using Suno since its launch, while taking its latest jab at the traditional music industry.
“The people making music on Suno are showing us what’s possible. Not institutional forecasts or industry narratives, but the lived reality of millions of creators: producers pushing boundaries, songwriters finding breakthroughs, new artists building careers,” claimed the company.
Well, quite. An alternative take on today’s news is that $250m is a sizeable amount of money that could be partially put to use signing licensing deals with those big, traditional music-industry companies.
Rival (and fellow sued-by-the-labels firm) Udio
struck a deal with Universal Music Group recently
. It’s valid to wonder whether Suno’s funding round could spur its own settlements and agreements in the coming weeks.
The funding caps a busy few months for Suno. Some of our recent coverage includes:
Upgrading its editing features in June
, then
acquiring browser-based DAW firm WavTool
later that month. This tech would form the core of the
Suno Studio software released in September.
Hiring veteran major-label executive Paul Sinclair as its chief music officer
in July.
Adding features
for users to add their own vocals and instrumentals
to their projects, also in July.
Unveiling a major update –
v5
– in September, claiming it was “the world’s best music model”, then
upgrading its v4.5 model for free users
in October.
Launching
its latest legal fightback against the major labels
that same month, before being
sued by Danish collecting society Koda in early November
.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
